title: United States-Cuba Trade Act of 2025
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
plain_summary: This bill would end the trade embargo between the United States and Cuba that has been in place for many years. It would cancel laws that stopped Americans from doing business with Cuba or traveling there freely. The bill would allow normal trade between the two countries, let Americans visit Cuba without restrictions, and permit phone and internet services between the nations. It would also allow people to send money back and forth between the United States and Cuba.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
subjects: Presidents and presidential powers, Vice Presidents

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the United States-Cuba Trade Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Removal of Provisions Restricting Trade and Other Relations with Cuba
sections.text: This section removes many laws that have restricted trade and business with Cuba. First, it cancels a section of the Foreign Assistance Act from nineteen sixty one that gave the government power to block aid and trade with Cuba. It also removes a reference to this canceled section from another law about court cases. Second, it stops the President from using powers under the Trading With the Enemy Act to control trade with Cuba. Any rules that were made using these powers will no longer be in effect. Third, it removes any bans on selling goods to Cuba that exist under export control laws. However, the President can still create new export controls or declare emergencies about Cuba in the future if there are new unusual threats to America that did not exist when this law was passed. Fourth, this section completely cancels the Cuban Democracy Act of nineteen ninety two. Fifth, it completely cancels the Cuban Liberty and Democratic Solidarity Act of nineteen ninety six, which was also called the LIBERTAD Act. It also removes parts of other laws that referred to the LIBERTAD Act. Sixth, it changes a law about aid to former Soviet countries by removing parts that mentioned Cuba's military and intelligence facilities. Seventh, it changes the Trade Sanctions Reform and Export Enhancement Act of two thousand to remove restrictions specific to Cuba. Eighth, it cancels a law that blocked certain transactions involving United States intellectual property and Cuba. Finally, it removes a provision that prohibited sugar quotas related to Cuba.

sections.title: Section 3  Telecommunications Equipment and Facilities
sections.text: Any common carrier (a company that provides communication services to the public) can install, maintain, and repair phone and internet equipment in Cuba. These companies can also provide communication services between the United States and Cuba. This includes the right to upgrade existing equipment and facilities.

sections.title: Section 4  Travel
sections.text: United States citizens and residents can travel to and from Cuba without government restrictions, as long as their travel would be legal within the United States. The government cannot regulate or prohibit normal transactions that go along with this travel. These normal travel transactions include expenses for staying in Cuba, regular banking transactions using foreign money drafts, traveler's checks, or other payment methods that are typical for travel.

sections.title: Section 5  Negotiations with Cuba
sections.text: The President should take all necessary steps to start talks with the Cuban government for two main purposes. First, to settle claims by United States citizens against Cuba for property that Cuba's government took from them. Second, to make sure Cuba protects internationally recognized human rights. In this section, a United States citizen means someone who is a citizen of the United States, and property means the same thing it means in another law called the International Claims Settlement Act.

sections.title: Section 6  Extension of Nondiscriminatory Trade Treatment
sections.text: Congress believes the United States should encourage democratic change and economic reform by making trade relations with Cuba normal. Congress also believes that once this law passes, America will no longer need to use a special trade rule called article twenty one of GATT nineteen ninety four against Cuba, though the President can still use this rule in the future if needed. This section changes the Harmonized Tariff Schedule (the list that shows what taxes to charge on imported goods) to remove Cuba from countries that face higher taxes. It cancels a nineteen sixty two law that imposed special tariff rules on Cuba. Most importantly, it gives Cuba normal trade relations treatment, meaning Cuban products will be taxed the same as products from other countries that America trades with normally. It also stops applying Title Four of the Trade Act of nineteen seventy four to Cuba, which was a law that restricted trade with certain countries. These changes will apply to goods that enter the United States fifteen days after this law is enacted. The President must send Congress a report about trade relations between America and Cuba within eighteen months after this law passes.

sections.title: Section 7  Prohibition on Limiting Annual Remittances
sections.text: The Secretary of the Treasury cannot limit how much money people under United States jurisdiction can send to Cuba. The Secretary must cancel all existing rules that limit these money transfers. However, this does not prevent the government from prosecuting people who commit money laundering crimes or who use money that came from illegal activities.

sections.title: Section 8  Requirement to Report to Congress Prior to Denial of Foreign Tax Credit with Respect to Certain Foreign Countries
sections.text: This section changes tax law so that before the President can deny foreign tax credits (tax breaks for businesses operating overseas) for any country, the President must first report to Congress that the country has been determined to meet certain negative criteria. This change applies to any determination about foreign countries made after this law passes, but it does not change the status of countries that were already denied these tax credits before this law was enacted.

sections.title: Section 9  Effective Date
sections.text: Most of this law and its changes will take effect sixty days after it is enacted. However, the sections about trade treatment and foreign tax credits have their own specific effective dates listed in those sections.
